stem: You are the community mental health registrar reviewing a 24-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia who started clozapine 8 weeks ago. He has tolerated the titration with mild constipation and sedation. He returns today for routine review. He reports no fever, sore throat or mouth ulcers. He has had blood tests done weekly since commencement under the central monitoring system.

data_table.Examination: Alert, no oropharyngeal lesions, no cervical lymphadenopathy, chest clear, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Latest FBC: Hb 144 g/L, WCC 6.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 3.4 x10^9/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L. Previous weekly results all within green-traffic-light range.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing haematological monitoring schedule?

options.text: Continue weekly full blood examinations for the duration of his clozapine treatment because the agranulocytosis risk does not change over the course of the first year
options.rationale: Risk of clozapine-induced neutropenia falls after the first 18 weeks; weekly testing for the entire treatment duration is unnecessary and not part of the Australian monitoring schedule. (Source: ClopineCentral and ClozaCare Monitoring Schedules)

options.text: Continue weekly full blood examinations until 18 weeks of treatment, then space to fortnightly until 12 months and monthly thereafter while clozapine is continued
options.rationale: Australian clozapine monitoring requires weekly FBE for the first 18 weeks of treatment, fortnightly to 12 months and then monthly while clozapine continues; this matches the time-course of agranulocytosis risk. (Source: ClopineCentral and ClozaCare Monitoring Schedules)

options.text: Reduce to monthly full blood examinations now that he has completed 8 weeks because his neutrophil counts have been stable through the first two months of treatment
options.rationale: Monthly monitoring is not introduced until after 12 months of treatment in Australia; the agranulocytosis risk peaks between weeks 6 and 18, so monthly testing at 8 weeks is unsafe. (Source: ClopineCentral and ClozaCare Monitoring Schedules)

options.text: Stop routine full blood examinations and rely on patient-reported symptoms of fever, sore throat or mucosal ulcers to trigger any further investigations during clozapine treatment
options.rationale: Routine FBE monitoring is mandatory under the Australian clozapine monitoring scheme; symptom-only monitoring misses early neutropenia and is not permitted. (Source: ClopineCentral and ClozaCare Monitoring Schedules)

options.text: Add weekly C-reactive protein and procalcitonin to the existing full blood examination to capture early signs of cytokine-mediated myocarditis during titration through this period of treatment
options.rationale: Cardiac surveillance with troponin and C-reactive protein is recommended in the first month for myocarditis screening, not weekly thereafter, and is separate from the haematological monitoring schedule. (Source: ClopineCentral and ClozaCare Monitoring Schedules)

explanation.summary: Clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic for treatment-resistant schizophrenia but carries a 0.4 to 0.9% risk of agranulocytosis. The Australian central monitoring system (ClopineCentral, ClozaCare) mandates weekly full blood examinations for the first 18 weeks, fortnightly to 12 months, and monthly thereafter. Dispensing is contingent on a result-positive flag within the monitoring system. Myocarditis screening with troponin and C-reactive protein is performed during the first 4 weeks because of a separate cardiac risk.
key_points: Agranulocytosis risk peaks between weeks 6 and 18 of clozapine treatment
Australian monitoring: weekly to week 18, fortnightly to 12 months, monthly thereafter
Red traffic light is neutrophils under 1.5 x10^9/L and requires clozapine cessation
Cardiac monitoring with troponin and CRP screens for myocarditis in the first month
Constipation can progress to ileus, which is a leading cause of clozapine-related death
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-induced constipation is dose-related and managed with osmotic laxatives such as macrogol from initiation; stimulant laxatives are added if symptoms persist.

subtopic_detail: First-line psychological therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 33-year-old woman with intrusive memories, nightmares, hypervigilance and avoidance for the past six months. The symptoms began after a serious motor vehicle accident in which a passenger died. She avoids driving and feels emotionally numb. She has no current suicidal ideation, no psychosis and no alcohol or substance misuse. She has not been on any psychotropic medication.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: anxious affect, congruent mood, intact thought form, no perceptual disturbance, future-oriented
data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 score 48 of 80. PHQ-9 score 12 of 27.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy with a clinical psychologist over 8 to 12 weekly sessions and review her response in primary care after the course
options.rationale: Trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy and eye movement desensitisation reprocessing are first-line in Australian PTSD guidelines for adults; psychological therapy is preferred over pharmacotherapy as the initial intervention. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Start oral diazepam 5 mg three times daily for symptomatic control of hyperarousal and review at four weeks for response and tolerance with possible dose adjustments
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines do not improve PTSD outcomes, may worsen the trauma response and risk dependence; they are not recommended as monotherapy or first-line. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Commence quetiapine 50 mg at night for sleep and intrusive symptoms with planned uptitration to 200 mg over 4 weeks if tolerated by the patient at clinical review
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotics are used as augmentation in treatment-resistant PTSD, not as a first-line monotherapy; first-line is psychological therapy. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Provide supportive psychoeducation only and review in 12 weeks because spontaneous remission occurs in most adults within the first six months of post-trauma symptoms
options.rationale: Symptoms persisting beyond one month with significant distress meet PTSD criteria and warrant active first-line therapy; watchful waiting alone is not appropriate at six months. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Start mirtazapine 30 mg at night and refer for generic supportive counselling sessions in the community over a course of weekly meetings spanning the next several months
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling is not trauma-focused and shows inferior outcomes compared with trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy; pharmacotherapy alone is also not first-line. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Post-traumatic stress disorder is diagnosed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions and hyperarousal symptoms following trauma. Australian guidelines from Phoenix Australia recommend trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy or eye movement desensitisation reprocessing as first-line treatment for adults. Pharmacotherapy with a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor or venlafaxine is considered when psychological therapy is unavailable, declined, or insufficient. Benzodiazepines and stand-alone supportive counselling are explicitly not recommended.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR are first-line psychological therapies for adult PTSD
Sertraline, paroxetine and venlafaxine are first-line pharmacotherapies if medication is required
Benzodiazepines are not recommended because they impair fear extinction
Atypical antipsychotics are used as augmentation in treatment-resistant PTSD only
Comorbid depression and substance use are treated concurrently to avoid undertreatment
explanation.pearls: PTSD with complex presentations involving prolonged interpersonal trauma may meet criteria for complex PTSD in ICD-11 and benefits from phased therapy with stabilisation before trauma-focused processing.

subtopic_detail: Long-term lithium monitoring schedule for renal and thyroid function
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has been on lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte for 4 years. She is euthymic and continues to work full time. She takes no other regular medications and has no recent illnesses. Her last formal lithium level monitoring was nine months ago. She asks how often she needs ongoing tests.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mood and affect, no tremor at rest, no peripheral oedema, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Latest serum lithium 0.7 mmol/L (12-hour trough), TSH 2.4 mIU/L, creatinine 68 micromol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73 m^2, corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing monitoring schedule?

options.text: Repeat serum lithium, urea and electrolytes and thyroid function tests now and then again every three months for the foreseeable course of treatment with this medication
options.rationale: Three-monthly monitoring is the initial schedule but not required indefinitely; once levels are stable, monitoring extends to six-monthly intervals. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stop routine monitoring because she has been stable for four years and lithium levels have remained therapeutic at the most recent 12-hour trough level measurement
options.rationale: Routine surveillance must continue lifelong because of cumulative renal, thyroid and parathyroid toxicity; stopping monitoring is not safe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Order serum lithium, urea, electrolytes, calcium and thyroid function every six months and an annual eGFR, with prompt repeat if dose, illness or new medications change
options.rationale: Six-monthly serum lithium, electrolytes, calcium and thyroid function with annual eGFR is the Australian long-term monitoring schedule for stable patients on lithium; additional testing is triggered by dose, illness or interacting medication changes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch to lamotrigine 200 mg daily as long-term maintenance therapy because lithium causes inevitable progression to chronic kidney disease over more than 5 years of continuous use
options.rationale: Long-term lithium does not inevitably cause clinically significant chronic kidney disease in most patients; switching to lamotrigine in a euthymic patient with normal renal function is not indicated. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Continue annual serum lithium only because all other organ-system tests for this medication can be reasonably deferred when the patient is clinically euthymic and well
options.rationale: Annual lithium alone misses thyroid, parathyroid and renal complications; renal and thyroid screening at least 6-monthly remains the Australian standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Lithium remains the most effective long-term treatment for bipolar I disorder and is the only mood stabiliser with a clear anti-suicidal effect. Long-term toxicity affects the thyroid (hypothyroidism in around 20%), parathyroid (hypercalcaemia in around 10%) and kidney (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and slowly progressive chronic kidney disease). The Australian monitoring schedule pairs 6-monthly lithium with electrolytes, calcium and thyroid function, plus annual eGFR. Any dose, illness, diuretic or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory change triggers an interim level.
key_points: Therapeutic range is 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance and 0.8 to 1.2 mmol/L for acute mania
Monitoring: 6-monthly lithium, urea, electrolytes, calcium and thyroid; annual eGFR
Hypothyroidism occurs in around 20% and hypercalcaemia in around 10% on long-term lithium
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is the most common renal adverse effect
Drug interactions: non-steroidal anti-inflammatories, ACE inhibitors and thiazides raise levels
explanation.pearls: A 50% rise in lithium level with the introduction of a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory is common; advise patients to use paracetamol preferentially and to alert their prescriber before any new medication.

subtopic_detail: Opioid agonist therapy initial agent choice and safety
stem: You are the addiction medicine registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man with heroin use disorder for the past three years. He injects daily and wants to start opioid agonist therapy. He has hepatitis C antibody positive, but liver function and synthetic function are preserved. He has had no overdoses but has been using alone. He has no chronic respiratory disease or current benzodiazepine use. He has unstable housing and limited transport.

data_table.Examination: Mild withdrawal symptoms with dilated pupils, piloerection and yawning; no track marks of skin and soft tissue infection
data_table.Investigations: ALT 48 U/L, AST 52 U/L, bilirubin 14 micromol/L, INR 1.0, albumin 38 g/L. Urine toxicology positive for heroin; negative for benzodiazepines and methadone.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial opioid agonist therapy?

options.text: Start oral methadone 80 mg daily today and continue at this dose until next review with daily supervised dosing at his community pharmacy of usual choice
options.rationale: Methadone induction starts at 20 to 30 mg with cautious titration over weeks because of risk of accumulation and overdose; starting at 80 mg risks fatal respiratory depression. (Source: National Guidelines for MATOD)

options.text: Trial extended-release oral naltrexone 50 mg daily from tomorrow for relapse prevention because of its low risk of accumulation and once-daily oral dosing convenience
options.rationale: Naltrexone requires sustained opioid abstinence for 7 to 10 days before initiation to avoid precipitated withdrawal; it is not appropriate for a person currently using heroin daily. (Source: National Guidelines for MATOD)

options.text: Start oral diazepam 10 mg three times daily and oral clonidine for autonomic features and arrange community-based residential rehabilitation in the next available place
options.rationale: Detoxification without agonist therapy has high relapse and overdose rates; benzodiazepines plus opioids carry overdose risk. (Source: National Guidelines for MATOD)

options.text: Defer pharmacotherapy and refer to weekly cognitive behaviour therapy and peer support group meetings as the initial treatment of his opioid use disorder before any agonist therapy
options.rationale: Opioid agonist therapy is first-line for opioid use disorder and reduces mortality by around 50%; deferring it for psychological therapy alone is not best practice. (Source: National Guidelines for MATOD)

options.text: Induce sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone 4 mg when he is in objective withdrawal, uptitrate to 8 to 16 mg over the first day and arrange a depot formulation when stable
options.rationale: Sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone is the preferred initial agonist for most patients in Australia because of its safety ceiling and overdose protection; long-acting depot buprenorphine improves adherence in unstable housing. (Source: National Guidelines for MATOD)

explanation.summary: Opioid agonist therapy with buprenorphine or methadone reduces all-cause mortality by around 50% in people with opioid use disorder. Sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone is preferred initially because of its partial agonist ceiling effect, lower respiratory depression risk and intrinsic abuse deterrent through naloxone. Long-acting depot buprenorphine (weekly or monthly subcutaneous formulations) is particularly useful for unstable housing or poor adherence. Methadone is reserved for patients with high tolerance, failed buprenorphine response or specific clinical contexts.
key_points: Buprenorphine-naloxone is preferred initially because of its ceiling effect and lower overdose risk
Induction requires objective opioid withdrawal (COWS at least 12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal
Depot buprenorphine improves adherence in unstable housing and reduces takeaway diversion risk
Methadone induction starts at 20 to 30 mg with cautious uptitration over weeks
Naltrexone requires 7 to 10 days of opioid abstinence before initiation
explanation.pearls: Naloxone is offered to every patient at induction and to their household contacts; intranasal naloxone is now subsidised on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme for take-home use in Australia.

subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose with QRS widening
stem: You are the emergency department registrar called to a 22-year-old woman brought in 90 minutes after ingesting 60 of her grandmother's amitriptyline 25 mg tablets. She is now drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 11. The paramedics have placed an intravenous cannula. There are no co-ingestants identified. She is otherwise well with no chronic disease.

data_table.Vital signs: T 35.9 °C, HR 124, BP 92/56, RR 22, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dilated pupils, dry mucous membranes, hypoactive bowel sounds, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 124, QRS 132 ms, terminal R wave in aVR 4 mm. VBG: pH 7.30, pCO2 32 mmHg, HCO3 17 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L. Na 137, K 3.4 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 1 to 2 mmol/kg as a slow bolus targeting QRS narrowing and arterial pH between 7.45 and 7.55, with intubation if airway protection deteriorates
options.rationale: QRS at or above 100 ms with hypotension and a positive aVR R wave indicates cardiotoxicity from sodium channel blockade; hypertonic sodium bicarbonate overcomes this and is the time-critical intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Start a continuous noradrenaline infusion at 0.1 microgram/kg/min and titrate up to maintain a mean arterial pressure above 65 mmHg without giving any specific sodium channel agent
options.rationale: Vasopressors may be required as an adjunct, but sodium bicarbonate is first-line to address sodium channel blockade and QRS prolongation directly; vasopressors alone do not correct conduction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer 50 g of oral activated charcoal slurry via nasogastric tube and admit for observation, deferring intravenous antidote until the patient stabilises clinically over the next hour
options.rationale: Activated charcoal at 90 minutes post-ingestion has limited value, particularly in a drowsy patient with airway risk; bicarbonate must not be deferred for adjunct gastrointestinal decontamination. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg increments up to a maximum of 1 mg to reverse the reduced consciousness and re-evaluate the cardiotoxicity after she becomes more alert
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not tricyclic toxicity, and may precipitate seizures in mixed overdoses, worsening acidosis and cardiotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Give intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g bolus and amiodarone 150 mg over 10 minutes to address the tachycardia and prevent ventricular tachyarrhythmia in a patient with tricyclic toxicity
options.rationale: Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in tricyclic toxicity because they worsen sodium channel blockade; magnesium and amiodarone are not first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes cardiotoxicity through sodium channel blockade, anticholinergic effects, and alpha blockade. The triad of QRS prolongation, hypotension and reduced consciousness is the classical presentation. Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate is the time-critical intervention because it overcomes sodium channel blockade through both sodium loading and alkalinisation. Lipid emulsion therapy is considered in refractory cardiotoxicity. Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated because they exacerbate conduction block.
key_points: QRS at or above 100 ms predicts seizure and at or above 160 ms predicts ventricular dysrhythmia
A terminal R wave in aVR above 3 mm is a sensitive marker of sodium channel blockade
Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate targeting arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55 narrows QRS
Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated and worsen conduction block
Intravenous lipid emulsion is considered for refractory cardiotoxicity
explanation.pearls: Hyperventilation alone after intubation can briefly reverse cardiotoxicity by raising arterial pH; this is a temporising measure while preparing sodium bicarbonate boluses.
